Big Dipper.description: The seven-star asterism in Ursa Major (Great Bear), one of the most familiar shapes in the northern sky. Dubhe and Merak are the 'pointers' that aim toward Polaris.

Northern Cross.description: The cruciform asterism of Cygnus the Swan, with Deneb at the tail and Albireo at the beak. One leg of the Summer Triangle along with Vega and Altair.

Andromeda.description: the Chained Maiden. The Chained Princess. A chain of three bright stars — Alpheratz, Mirach, Almach — runs away from the Great Square of Pegasus.

Antlia.description: The Air Pump, a faint southern constellation created by Nicolas-Louis de Lacaille in the 18th century.

Apus.description: The Bird of Paradise, a faint southern constellation near the south celestial pole.

Aquila.description: The Eagle, with Altair at the head flanked by Tarazed and Alshain. Altair is the twelfth-brightest star in the sky and one corner of the Summer Triangle.

Aquarius.description: the Water Bearer. The Water-Bearer, one of the oldest recognized constellations. Sadalmelik and Sadalsuud are the two luckiest stars in the sky by traditional Arabic names.

Ara.description: The Altar, a small southern constellation between Scorpius and Triangulum Australe.

Aries.description: The Ram. A small zodiacal constellation marked by a simple arc of three stars: Hamal, Sheratan, and Mesarthim.

Auriga.description: The Charioteer, a large pentagon of bright stars sharing Elnath with Taurus. Capella, the sixth-brightest star in the sky, dominates.

Boötes.description: The Herdsman, a kite-shaped constellation anchored by brilliant orange Arcturus, the fourth-brightest star in the sky.

Canes Venatici.description: The Hunting Dogs, representing the dogs of Boötes. Cor Caroli is named in honor of Charles I of England.

Camelopardalis.description: The Giraffe, a large but faint constellation in the north circumpolar region near Polaris.

Capricornus.description: the Sea Goat. The Sea-Goat, a faint but ancient zodiacal figure forming a rough triangle in the southern sky.

Carina.description: The Keel of the ship Argo. Canopus is the second-brightest star in the night sky.

Cassiopeia.description: The Queen, a distinctive 'W' (or 'M' depending on orientation) of five bright stars in the northern circumpolar sky.

Centaurus.description: The Centaur, home to the closest stellar system to Sol (Alpha Centauri) and the bright blue giant Hadar.

Cepheus.description: The King, a house-shaped constellation near the north celestial pole.

Cetus.description: The Sea Monster (or Whale), a large sprawling southern constellation from the Perseus myth.

Chamaeleon.description: The Chameleon, a small faint constellation near the south celestial pole.

Cancer.description: The Crab, a faint zodiacal constellation between Gemini and Leo. Home to the Beehive Cluster (M44).

Columba.description: The Dove, a constellation south of Lepus representing the dove sent by Noah or the dove that guided the Argonauts.

Corona Australis.description: The Southern Crown, a small constellation below Sagittarius forming a gentle arc of faint stars.

Corona Borealis.description: The Northern Crown, a small semicircular arc of stars. Alphecca (Gemma) is its brightest member.

Crater.description: The Cup, a faint constellation sitting on the back of Hydra, next to Corvus.

Crux.description: The Southern Cross, the smallest constellation but one of the most recognizable, depicted on several southern-hemisphere flags. Acrux and Gacrux form the long axis; Mimosa and Imai form the cross-bar.

Corvus.description: The Crow, a small but distinctive quadrilateral of four moderately bright stars sitting on the back of Hydra.

Cygnus.description: The Swan, flying along the Milky Way with Deneb at the tail and Albireo at the head. Contains the Northern Cross asterism and forms part of the Summer Triangle.

Delphinus.description: The Dolphin, a small diamond-shaped constellation. Sualocin and Rotanev encode Nicolaus Venator (Niccolò Cacciatore) when read backwards.

Dorado.description: the Dolphinfish. The Swordfish (or Dolphinfish), a southern constellation containing the Large Magellanic Cloud.

Draco.description: The Dragon, a long winding circumpolar constellation whose tail once contained the pole star Thuban ~4,800 years ago.

Equuleus.description: the Pony. The Little Horse, the second-smallest constellation. Only its head is depicted in the sky.

Eridanus.description: The River, an enormous winding constellation beginning near Rigel (in Orion) and flowing down to brilliant Achernar in the far south.

Fornax.description: The Furnace, a faint constellation south of Cetus. Contains the Fornax Cluster, a nearby galaxy cluster.

Gemini.description: The Twins, with the heads of Castor and Pollux side by side and two parallel lines of stars trailing off representing their bodies.

Grus.description: The Crane, a southern constellation introduced by Dutch navigators in the late 16th century.

Hercules.description: the Strongman. The Strong Man, a large but relatively faint constellation. The Keystone asterism (four stars forming a trapezoid) marks the torso.

Indus.description: The Indian, a faint constellation near the south celestial pole.

Lacerta.description: The Lizard, a faint zigzag of stars between Cygnus and Andromeda.

Leo.description: The Lion, with the curved 'Sickle' asterism marking the lion's mane and head, and a triangle of stars (Denebola, Zosma, Chertan) marking the hindquarters and tail.

Lepus.description: The Hare, crouching at the feet of Orion.

Libra.description: The Scales, the only zodiac constellation named for an object rather than a creature. Zubenelgenubi ('southern claw') and Zubeneschamali ('northern claw') recall its former role as part of Scorpius.

Lupus.description: The Wolf, a constellation south of Libra and Scorpius in a rich area of the Milky Way.

Lynx.description: The Lynx, a faint constellation between Ursa Major and Auriga. Said to require the eyes of a lynx to see it.

Lyra.description: The Lyre, a small but bright constellation dominated by Vega — the brightest star in the northern celestial hemisphere and one corner of the Summer Triangle.

Mensa.description: Table Mountain, the faintest of all 88 constellations, named after Table Mountain in South Africa where Lacaille observed.

Microscopium.description: The Microscope, a faint southern constellation between Capricornus and Piscis Austrinus.

Monoceros.description: The Unicorn, a large but faint constellation in the winter Milky Way between Canis Major and Gemini. Contains the Rosette Nebula.

Musca.description: The Fly, a small constellation south of Crux.

Norma.description: the Carpenter's Level. The Carpenter's Square, a faint constellation in a rich area of the Milky Way near Scorpius.

Octans.description: The Octant, the constellation containing the south celestial pole. Its stars are too faint for a prominent pattern.

Ophiuchus.description: the Serpent Bearer. The Serpent-Bearer, a large constellation occasionally called the 13th zodiac sign since the ecliptic passes through it.

Orion.description: The Hunter, one of the most recognizable constellations. Anchored by the bright shoulders Betelgeuse and Bellatrix, the feet Rigel and Saiph, and the three-star Belt Mintaka–Alnilam–Alnitak.

Pavo.description: The Peacock, a southern constellation named by Dutch navigators. Its brightest star is simply called Peacock.

Pegasus.description: The Winged Horse, marked by the Great Square (Markab, Scheat, Alpheratz, Algenib) forming the body. Enif marks the horse's nose.

Perseus.description: The Hero, holding the severed head of Medusa (Algol, the 'Demon Star', a famous eclipsing binary). Mirfak anchors the central curve.

Phoenix.description: The Phoenix, a southern constellation representing the mythological bird that rises from its own ashes.

Piscis Austrinus.description: The Southern Fish, a small constellation whose lone bright star Fomalhaut is one of the brightest in the sky and hosts a famous debris disk.

Pisces.description: The Fishes, a large but faint zodiacal constellation. The two fish are connected by a 'cord' meeting at the knot star Alrescha.

Puppis.description: the Poop Deck. The Stern (or Poop Deck) of the ship Argo, rich in clusters and nestled in the Milky Way.

Reticulum.description: The Reticle (telescope crosshairs), a small faint constellation near the Large Magellanic Cloud.

Sculptor.description: The Sculptor, a faint constellation south of Aquarius. Contains the Sculptor Galaxy (NGC 253) and the south galactic pole.

Scorpius.description: The Scorpion, with red supergiant Antares at its heart and a long curving tail ending in the stinger stars Shaula and Lesath. One of the few constellations that genuinely resembles its namesake.

Scutum.description: The Shield, a small constellation in the Milky Way south of Aquila, containing the dense Wild Duck Cluster (M11).

Serpens.description: The Serpent, the only constellation split into two parts: Serpens Caput (head) and Serpens Cauda (tail), separated by Ophiuchus.

Sextans.description: The Sextant, a faint constellation on the celestial equator between Leo and Hydra.

Sagitta.description: The Arrow, the third-smallest constellation, sitting in the Milky Way between Vulpecula and Aquila.

Sagittarius.description: The Archer, whose brightest stars form the famous 'Teapot' asterism pointed toward the center of the Milky Way.

Taurus.description: The Bull, with the V-shaped Hyades cluster forming the face, red giant Aldebaran as the glaring eye, and two long horns ending at Elnath and Tianguan.

Telescopium.description: The Telescope, a faint constellation between Sagittarius and Pavo.

Triangulum Australe.description: The Southern Triangle, a small bright constellation near Alpha Centauri, easily visible in the southern sky.

Triangulum.description: The Triangle, a small northern constellation. Contains the Triangulum Galaxy (M33), the third-largest galaxy in the Local Group.

Tucana.description: The Toucan, a southern constellation containing the Small Magellanic Cloud and the globular cluster 47 Tucanae.

Ursa Major.description: The Great Bear, one of the largest constellations. Contains the Big Dipper asterism and extends south with the bear's legs and body.

Vela.description: The Sails of the ship Argo, forming part of the 'False Cross' asterism often mistaken for the Southern Cross.

Virgo.description: The Maiden, the second-largest constellation in the sky. Anchored by the blue supergiant Spica, with Porrima and Vindemiatrix extending the wheat-sheaf the figure carries.

Volans.description: The Flying Fish, a faint constellation between Carina and the Large Magellanic Cloud.

Vulpecula.description: The Little Fox, a faint constellation in the Milky Way between Sagitta and Cygnus. Contains the Dumbbell Nebula (M27).
